positioning: Instant land records and title search, digitally
description: Landeed lets buyers, lawyers, and developers instantly pull land records, encumbrance certificates, and title documents from government portals across India — without visiting sub-registrar offices. Available for Telangana, AP, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, and expanding.
key_problem: Land title search in India requires physical visits to sub-registrar offices; documents are in regional languages and hard to interpret. Landeed digitises access and provides plain-English summaries.
revenue_model: Per-report fees (₹99–₹999 per document pull), monthly API subscription for law firms and banks
geography: India — South focus (Telangana, AP, Karnataka, TN)
differentiator: API-first; only player with direct government portal integration for instant EC pulls
weakness: Coverage limited to ~5 states; no soil/environment or demand analytics layer
notes: YC alumni. Closest direct competitor to KYL on the title-search layer. Does not do holistic land reports (no soil, air, demand growth).

positioning: India's largest real estate data analytics platform for developers, investors, and banks
description: PropEquity aggregates RERA, IGRS, and listing data across 45+ cities to provide demand analytics, price trends, project completion risk, and competitive intelligence. Used by developers (pre-launch pricing), banks (appraisal risk), and PE funds (acquisition decisions).
key_problem: Developers and investors making multi-crore land and project decisions with incomplete market data. PropEquity provides city/micro-market level supply-demand analytics.
revenue_model: Annual SaaS subscriptions (₹5L–₹25L/yr per user segment: developer, bank, investor). Custom research reports on project basis.
differentiator: Deepest RERA data integration in India; 15+ years of historical price data across micro-markets
weakness: No title/legal layer; no physical attribute data (soil, environment). Expensive for individual buyers.
notes: Bootstrapped and profitable. Competes with KYL on demand analytics and market sizing data, but not on title search or physical land attributes.

positioning: India's oldest independent real estate research firm
description: Liases Foras provides quarterly residential real estate market reports covering 42 cities — unit launches, absorption rates, price indices, unsold inventory. Primary data collector for MoHUA's national housing statistics. Founder Pankaj Kapoor is a well-known media commentator on Indian real estate.
key_problem: No credible, independent source for India housing market data (free from developer PR bias). Liases Foras is the industry's reference for absorption and inventory data.
revenue_model: Subscription reports, custom research for government and NBFC clients, media licensing of data
differentiator: Trusted by MoHUA, NHB, and Supreme Court committees for housing data. Oldest and most cited.
weakness: No tech platform; PDF-report model. No land-specific focus. No title or physical attributes.
notes: More of a data licensor/research firm than a product. Competes with KYL only on market research reports for developers/investors.

positioning: Registered sale deed data for transparent property valuations in Mumbai
description: Zapkey pulls registered sale transactions from IGR Maharashtra and makes them searchable by project/building — enabling buyers to see actual transaction prices (not asking prices) in any building. Covers 30+ cities with verified transaction data.
key_problem: Indian real estate is opaque — asking prices on portals are inflated. Zapkey shows real sold prices from government registration records.
revenue_model: Freemium — basic search free, detailed reports ₹499. B2B API for banks and valuers.
differentiator: Building-level registered transaction history in consumer-friendly UI; fastest time-to-valuation
weakness: Residential apartments focus; no land/plot layer, no title search, no environmental data
notes: Narrower focus than KYL. Competes on price discovery but not on land due diligence.

positioning: Online property legal documents, title opinion, and registration assistance
description: LegalDesk provides online legal document drafting (sale deeds, NOCs, property agreements) and connects buyers with lawyers for title opinion and due diligence. Offers end-to-end property registration facilitation in select cities.
key_problem: Property legal documentation in India requires physical visits to lawyers and notaries. LegalDesk makes standard property legal work digital and accessible.
revenue_model: Per-document fees (₹500–₹5,000), fixed-fee legal packages (₹15,000–₹50,000 for full due diligence), lawyer marketplace commission
differentiator: Lawyer marketplace + document automation under one roof; registered with Bar Council
weakness: Human-powered title search (slow, inconsistent quality). No data analytics or market intelligence layer.
notes: Adjacent to KYL on due diligence but legal-services model vs. data-product model. Not a direct competitor.

positioning: Property verification and legal check as an add-on to NoBroker's rental/sale platform
description: NoBroker offers a property verification service where their team does a physical site visit, verifies ownership documents, checks for encumbrances, and delivers a report within 5–7 days. Priced at ₹3,500–₹7,500 per property.
key_problem: Buyers on NoBroker's platform face risk of fake listings and undisclosed encumbrances. Embedded verification reduces transaction drop-off.
revenue_model: One-time flat fee per property verification (₹3,500–₹7,500). Upsell to legal registration services.
geography: India — Bengaluru, Pune, Hyderabad, Mumbai, Delhi NCR, Chennai
differentiator: Embedded in transaction flow; brand trust from NoBroker's 10M+ user base
weakness: Human-powered, not scalable; no land-specific expertise (focuses on apartments). Primarily rental-market.
notes: NoBroker's verification is apartment-centric and embedded within their platform. Not a standalone land research product.

positioning: US land parcel data and boundary intelligence for developers, utilities, and government
description: Regrid is the leading US land parcel data provider — maintaining 150M+ parcel records with owner information, boundaries, zoning, and legal descriptions. Data sold via API, bulk download, and a self-serve map tool. Powers real estate, insurance, solar, telecom, and agriculture decisions.
key_problem: US parcel data is fragmented across 3,000+ county assessors. Regrid normalises and maintains it as a single queryable dataset.
revenue_model: API subscriptions ($500–$50,000/yr), bulk data licensing, enterprise deals
differentiator: Only nationwide US parcel dataset with consistent schema; API-first with offline download
weakness: US only. No applicability to India's fragmented land records system.
notes: Best international analogue for what KYL aims to build for India. Regrid = Indian KYL's north star product. US parcel data is more structured (Torrens system); India's land records far more fragmented.

positioning: US property data platform for lenders, title companies, and investors
description: DataTree is First American's property data platform providing ownership, lien, mortgage, and title plant data across 99% of the US population. Used by banks for origination, title insurers for risk assessment, and investors for deal sourcing. First American is a Fortune 500 title insurance company.
key_problem: Title and lien research in the US requires access to fragmented county records. DataTree aggregates 40+ years of title plant data into a searchable SaaS platform.
revenue_model: Subscription SaaS, per-report fees for consumers, bulk data licensing for enterprise
funding_stage: Part of First American Financial (NYSE: FAF, $6B revenue)
differentiator: Access to First American's 40-year title plant; deepest lien and encumbrance history in the US
weakness: US-only. Enterprise pricing. No environmental or demand analytics.
notes: First American earns ~$6B in revenue from title insurance + data. Illustrates how large the data layer of land transactions can become.

positioning: The Bloomberg of commercial real estate data — global property intelligence platform
description: CoStar provides the most comprehensive commercial real estate database in the world — covering 6M+ properties with sales comps, lease comps, availability, tenant data, and market analytics. Owns LoopNet (commercial listings), Apartments.com, Homes.com, and STR (hotel data). Listed on NASDAQ.
key_problem: Commercial real estate has no public price discovery mechanism. CoStar acts as the data backbone for every CRE transaction — every broker, investor, lender uses it.
revenue_model: Subscription SaaS ($500–$5,000/user/month for CoStar Professional). Advertising on LoopNet/Homes.com.
geography: USA, UK, Canada, France, Spain, Germany — expanding to India
differentiator: Largest proprietary dataset (500+ field researchers doing physical property verification). Network effects: everyone uses it, so everyone adds to it.
weakness: Extremely expensive. US/Europe focused — limited India data currently. Not land/plot specific.
notes: CoStar is the long-term aspiration for what a mature Indian RE data platform could become. ~$2.5B annual revenue. CoStar has announced India expansion intentions.

positioning: AI-powered digitisation of land records from physical documents and field surveys
description: Emerging player using OCR and ML to digitise land revenue records (RoR, patta, khata) from physical documents, helping state governments and NGOs create digital cadastral maps. Early stage with government pilot contracts in Karnataka and Madhya Pradesh.
key_problem: Over 60% of India's land records still exist only in physical form — handwritten in regional languages across taluk offices. AI-powered digitisation is required to build any national land data layer.
revenue_model: Government contract (SaaS + digitisation fee per village/district). Early-stage exploring B2B API.
differentiator: OCR specifically trained on Indian regional-language land documents (Kannada, Hindi)
weakness: Very early stage. Government procurement cycle is slow. No consumer-facing product.
notes: Infrastructure layer player — could become either a partner or a competitor depending on whether they build a consumer/B2B product on top of the digitised data.

positioning: Central registry for securitisation and reconstruction assets — India's only national lien database
description: CERSAI (Central Registry of Securitisation Asset Reconstruction and Security Interest) is the GOI-mandated registry where banks must register mortgages and security interests over immovable property. Any lender financing a property must search CERSAI first. Handles 4M+ registrations per year.
key_problem: Multiple banks financing the same property ('double-financing fraud') was a major NPA risk in India. CERSAI creates a central encumbrance register to prevent this.
revenue_model: Government entity — fee per search (₹50–₹500) and per registration (₹250–₹500). Revenue ~₹80 Cr/yr.
differentiator: Mandated by law (SARFAESI Act). Only authoritative source for national mortgage/lien data.
weakness: Data not freely accessible — requires paid search, no API for bulk/programmatic access. Only covers registered mortgages, not unregistered encumbrances.
notes: Not a competitor — a critical data source that KYL should integrate. CERSAI search is a standard step in any land due diligence checklist.

positioning: Satellite-based land intelligence — change detection, land use classification, crop monitoring
description: Orbital Insight uses satellite imagery and ML to track physical changes on land — construction progress, agricultural land-use change, deforestation, infrastructure. Serves commodity traders, hedge funds, insurance companies, and government agencies. Acquired by Maxar Technologies (satellite imagery provider).
key_problem: Physical asset monitoring at scale is impossible with human inspection. Satellite data + AI enables continuous monitoring of millions of land parcels.
revenue_model: Enterprise SaaS contracts ($100K–$10M/yr). Government defense contracts via Maxar parent.
differentiator: Access to Maxar's satellite constellation (30cm resolution); proven AI models for change detection
weakness: Expensive enterprise product ($100K+ contracts). No India-specific title/legal layer. Not consumer-accessible.
notes: Represents the geospatial intelligence angle that could augment KYL's physical land attribute data (soil quality, land use history, encroachment detection via satellite).
